title: The Life of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: A Complete Seerah Overview
excerpt: The Seerah — the biography of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ — is the most documented life in human history. This complete overview covers his birth, prophethood, migration, major events, and the lessons each phase holds for Muslims today.
author: QuranBookk
content:
Allah describes the Prophet ﷺ in the Quran with the highest possible honour:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ"
  translation="And indeed, you are of a great moral character."
  reference="Surah Al-Qalam 68:4"
/>

And He defines the purpose of this man's life with equal clarity:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَٰلَمِينَ"
  translation="And We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], except as a mercy to the worlds."
  reference="Surah Al-Anbiya 21:107"
/>

Muhammad ibn Abdullah ﷺ was born in Makkah in approximately 570 CE — the Year of the Elephant, named after the failed Abyssinian military expedition to destroy the Kaaba. He died 63 years later, having transformed a society of warring tribes practicing female infanticide into a civilisation built on justice, mercy, and knowledge. This is his story.

## Phase 1: Before Prophethood (570–610 CE)

### Birth and Early Childhood

Muhammad ﷺ was born into the clan of Banu Hashim, of the tribe of Quraysh — the custodians of the Kaaba and the most influential family in Arabia. His father, Abdullah, died before his birth. His mother, Aminah, died when he was six. He was raised by his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib, then — after Abd al-Muttalib's death — by his uncle Abu Talib. Following Arab custom, he was given to a wet nurse in the desert: Halimah al-Sa'diyya of the Banu Sa'd tribe, where he spent his first years. Even as an infant, those around him noted a quality of light about him.

### The Young Man

He worked as a shepherd — a role, the Prophet ﷺ later noted, that every prophet shared. At 25, he was employed by a wealthy widow named Khadijah bint Khuwaylid to lead a trade caravan to Syria. Impressed by his honesty and character, she proposed marriage. He accepted. She was 40; he was 25. Their marriage was the deepest love story of his life. He was known in Makkah as *Al-Amin* (the Trustworthy) and *Al-Sadiq* (the Truthful) — qualities even his enemies acknowledged throughout his life.

### The Cave of Hira

In his late thirties, he began spending extended periods in meditation in a cave on Mount Hira outside Makkah. He was searching — dissatisfied with the idolatry, female infanticide, oppression of the poor, and tribal warfare that defined his society.

## Phase 2: Prophethood in Makkah (610–622 CE)

### The First Revelation

At 40, in the cave of Hira during the month of Ramadan, the angel Jibril appeared:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="ٱقْرَأْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ"
  translation="Recite in the name of your Lord who created."
  reference="Surah Al-Alaq 96:1"
/>

The Prophet ﷺ returned to Khadijah, shaking. She held him, reassured him, and immediately affirmed his prophethood: *"Allah would never disgrace you. You uphold the ties of kinship, carry the burden of others, give to the poor, honour guests, and help those struck by calamity."* (Sahih al-Bukhari)

Khadijah was the first Muslim.

### The Years of Secret Calling (610–613 CE)

For three years, Islam spread quietly among close circles. The first believers: Khadijah (wife), Ali ibn Abi Talib (cousin, then a child), Zayd ibn Harithah (freed slave and adopted son), and Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (closest friend).

### Public Proclamation and Persecution (613–622 CE)

When Allah commanded the Prophet ﷺ to proclaim Islam publicly, Quraysh's response was swift and brutal — but directed almost entirely at the poor, the enslaved, and the socially unprotected. Bilal ibn Rabah — an Ethiopian slave — was tortured in the desert heat with rocks placed on his chest, crying *"Ahad, Ahad"* (One, One — affirming Allah's oneness). Abu Bakr bought and freed him.

The Prophet ﷺ himself was physically harassed, had garbage thrown on him in prayer, and was economically boycotted. Yet he never retaliated with violence.

**The Year of Grief (619 CE)**: Both Khadijah and Abu Talib died within months of each other. The Prophet ﷺ lost his greatest personal support and his tribal protection simultaneously.

**The Night Journey and Ascension (Isra wal-Miraj, 621 CE)**: Allah took His Prophet from Makkah to Jerusalem and then through the heavens. The five daily prayers were prescribed during this journey — a direct gift from Allah to the believers, without intermediary.

## Phase 3: The Hijra and Madinah (622–632 CE)

### The Migration (Hijra, 622 CE)

After 13 years of persecution with little material success, Allah permitted the Muslims to migrate to Yathrib (later renamed Madinah al-Munawwarah — the Radiant City). The Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr made the journey in secret, sheltering in the Cave of Thawr for three days while search parties from Quraysh came within feet of finding them.

<AyahBlock
  arabic="إِلَّا تَنصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُمَا فِى ٱلْغَارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَاحِبِهِۦ لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا"
  translation="If you do not aid him, Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us.'"
  reference="Surah At-Tawbah 9:40"
/>

The Islamic calendar begins from this moment — not from the birth of the Prophet ﷺ or his first revelation, but from the migration. It marks the beginning of the Muslim community as a social reality.

### Building the Muslim Community in Madinah

In Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ accomplished things no purely military or political leader could:

- **Built Masjid al-Nabawi** — the Prophet's Mosque, which remains one of the holiest sites on earth
- **Established the Constitution of Madinah** — a written agreement between Muslims, Jews, and other tribes defining rights and obligations
- **Instituted the brotherhood (mu'akhat)** — pairing each Muhajir (migrant from Makkah) with an Ansar (helper from Madinah) who shared his home, wealth, and life

### The Major Battles

**Battle of Badr (624 CE)**: 313 poorly equipped Muslims defeated an army of 1,000. The Prophet ﷺ spent the entire night before the battle in prayer.

**Battle of Uhud (625 CE)**: A near-defeat caused by a tactical error from archers who abandoned their position. The Prophet ﷺ was injured. Yet even in this moment, when asked to curse the disbelievers, he prayed: *"O Allah, guide my people, for they do not know."*

**Battle of the Trench (627 CE)**: An alliance of 10,000 besieged Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ accepted Salman al-Farisi's suggestion to dig a defensive trench — an unprecedented tactic in Arabia. The siege lasted weeks before the alliance collapsed.

### The Conquest of Makkah (630 CE)

Eight years after fleeing in secret, the Prophet ﷺ returned to Makkah with 10,000 Muslims. The city that had persecuted, boycotted, and driven out the early Muslims surrendered without significant bloodshed. He stood at the Kaaba and declared a general amnesty for his enemies — including Abu Sufyan, who had led armies against him. He said: *"Go, for you are free."*

### The Farewell Pilgrimage and Final Sermon (632 CE)

In his final Hajj, the Prophet ﷺ addressed 100,000 pilgrims with words that remain Islam's charter of human rights:

<HadithBlock
  text="O people! Your Lord is one and your father is one. All of you are from Adam, and Adam was from dust. There is no superiority for an Arab over a non-Arab, nor for a non-Arab over an Arab, nor for a white over a black, nor a black over a white — except by taqwa (righteousness)."
  narrator="Abu Nadrah"
  source="Musnad Ahmad 23536 — Authenticated by al-Albani"
/>

He died in Madinah three months later, his head resting in the lap of Aisha (رضي الله عنها), saying: *"To the highest companion"* — his last words, choosing Allah over this world.

## Why Study the Seerah?

The Seerah is not history for its own sake. Allah says:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="لَّقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِى رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ"
  translation="There has certainly been for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent pattern of conduct."
  reference="Surah Al-Ahzab 33:21"
/>

Every phase of his life contains a lesson for our own. His patience in Makkah teaches us how to hold onto faith under pressure. His governance in Madinah teaches us how to build communities of justice. His treatment of enemies teaches us the power of forgiveness. His personal life teaches us how to love, grieve, laugh, and be human — while remaining anchored to Allah.

## Explore the Seerah Through Quran and Hadith on QuranBookk

The Seerah comes alive when read alongside the Quranic verses revealed during each phase. Many surahs were revealed in response to specific events in his life.

👉 [Read the Quran with the context of revelation on QuranBookk](/quran/surahs)

👉 [Explore the Hadith collections that preserve his words and actions](/hadiths/hadiths-sources)

👉 [Ask QuranBookk's Islam AI about specific events in the Seerah](/ask-islam-ai)

To know the Prophet ﷺ is to love him. And to love him is to follow him. That is the path to Allah.
